body.$value: Your only super admin is locked out of the Google Admin console. Which approach restores access the fastest?
Open a support escalation case and prove DNS control of the primary domain
Have another existing super admin sign in and reset the locked-out admin's password
Wait up to 24 hours for DNS records to propagate before trying again
Submit the user-promotion recovery form to request a new super admin
In the formal support escalation path, how does an organization prove it controls the primary domain, and how long can it take?
By adding a CNAME or TXT record at the DNS host; propagation can take up to 24 hours
By emailing a scanned copy of the domain registration invoice; verified within minutes
By replying from any address on the domain; verified instantly
By uploading an HTML file to the school website; verified within 24 hours
A district has lost access to its DNS host and domain registrar. What does this mean for the domain-ownership recovery path?
It is unaffected, since recovery verifies ownership by email instead of DNS
It still works, but propagation simply takes longer than 24 hours
The recovery path is blocked, because ownership cannot be proven without DNS/registrar control
It speeds up, because support can verify ownership through the registrar directly

True or False: On Education editions, the 'Allow super admins to recover their account' setting (Security > Authentication > Account recovery) is ON by default, so maintaining a second super admin is unnecessary.
True
False
Under what conditions does the 'user-promotion' recovery form (a domain user requesting promotion to super admin) actually work?
Any domain user can use it at any time to instantly grant themselves super admin
It works only after verifying domain ownership and only when existing admins are inactive or unresponsive
It works only if the requester already holds a delegated admin role
It works only on weekends when Google support manually reviews each request
